title: Spinning flax: wet-spun linen from top
subtitle: Drawing and spinning linen yarn from prepared flax top using the wet-spinning method
excerpt: Flax is a plant fibre with very different behaviour from wool: it does not have wool's elasticity, requires moisture to spin smoothly, and produces a dense, inelastic linen yarn. Wet spinning, in which the fingers are kept wet throughout to smooth the bast fibres together, is the traditional method for producing fine, even linen from prepared flax top.
sourceNotes: Hooper, Luther. 'Hand-Loom Weaving.' Pitman, 1910. Chapter on linen yarn production, particularly the preparation and wet-spinning of flax. Internet Archive: archive.org/details/handloomweaving00hoop. Also: Hummel, J.J. 'The Dyeing of Textile Fabrics.' Cassell, 1885. General textile fibre properties section.

content.text: : moisture softens the bast fibres and allows them to slide together. Without moisture, flax fibres resist drafting and produce a hairy, uneven singles. Flax is also rigid: it does not stretch as wool does, and broken sections cannot be joined by simply overlapping them. Take-up (twist) is high and the finished yarn is inelastic.

attrs.body: Keep hair, loose clothing, and cord away from the drive band and flyer. Tie back hair before starting.
attrs.title: Wheel safety

items.name: Small bowl of water for wet-spinning
Spinning wheel (high drive ratio for linen), scotch-tension preferred
Swift and ball winder (for skeining if needed)

content.text: Pull a length of flax top about 30 cm long from the bundle. Dampen it slightly by drawing it through the water bowl: not dripping wet, just damp. Flax fibres that enter the draft zone dry will produce breaks.

Use a high drive ratio (the small whorl, large pulley setting) for linen; flax requires more twist per length than wool to hold the fibres together. Scotch tension (single drive with a brake on the bobbin) gives more control over take-up than double drive. Set the brake spring tension to moderate: not so loose the bobbin spins freely, not so tight that drawing is difficult.

Wet the front two fingers of your downstream hand. Hold the flax top in your upstream hand. Draft the fibres forward with your wet downstream fingers, drawing them out slowly and evenly. The wet fingers smooth the fibres together as they pass. Treadle at a steady pace slightly faster than wool (more twist per unit length). Keep the water close and re-wet your fingers every 20-30 seconds.

Wind off onto the niddy-noddy. Tie the skein in four places with cotton thread (not wool: wool ties will felt). Boil the skein briefly (10 minutes in plain hot water, no detergent) to set the twist and soften the fibre. This is traditional linen finishing: the boiling relaxes the spun fibres and produces the characteristic smooth hand of linen. Hang to dry under slight tension to produce a smooth, slightly stiff yarn. The stiffness will ease with washing.

items.fix: Keep fibres wetter in the draft zone. Shorten the draft zone: flax fibres are long but must be drafted at shorter intervals than wool.
items.cause: Fibres too dry, or draft zone too long for the fibre staple length
items.problem: The singles break repeatedly during drafting

items.fix: Wet the fingers more frequently and increase drive ratio.
items.cause: Dry-spinning rather than wet-spinning, or insufficient twist per length
items.problem: The finished yarn is very hairy and rough

items.fix: Slow the treadle speed slightly or increase the draft zone. Test a short length by letting it hang in a loose loop.
items.cause: Over-twisted: too much twist per length
items.problem: The yarn is too stiff and wiry
